observations.recommendation:
a. Unadjusted net book reconciling items at year-end
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant trace the supporting documents of the identified book reconciling items, establish their validity, and immediately draw the necessary adjustments in the books. Once verified, appropriate verification and adjustments will be made in the books to reflect accurate balances.

b. Stale and unreleased checks totaling ₱5.805 million not reverted to CIB at year-end
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant revert the amount of the stale checks to the CIB account following the procedures prescribed under the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs.

We also reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Treasurer prepare the list of unclaimed checks at the end of the year and submit the same to the Municipal Accountant for preparation of JEV to restore the amount of unreleased checks to CIB, recognize the appropriate liability account every end of the year, and reverse the same in the ensuing year.

c. Unreconciled difference in cash balances between accounting records and cashbook
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant conduct frequent reconciliation at least quarterly and at year-end to address the discrepancies in the balances of cash accounts between the cashbook and ledger and apply necessary adjustments accordingly.

We recommended that the: 

a. Municipal Mayor shall issue a memorandum to strengthen the coordination between the Engineering and Accounting Offices to ensure that construction materials are correctly accounted for;

b. Municipal Accountant (i) reclassify the cost of materials amounting to ₱3.027 million from the CIP account to the Construction Materials Inventory account and stop the direct recognition of delivered construction materials to the CIP account until they are actually issued or utilized for the project, and (ii) recognize and accumulate all costs of projects to CIP accounts during construction, and reclassify them to their appropriate PPE accounts upon completion; and

c. Municipal Engineer and Municipal General Services Officer furnish the Municipal Accounting Office with SSMIs every week as bases for recording of issued materials, and update the status of projects implemented by-administration.

We reiterated our previous recommendations that the: 

a. Municipal Accountant and GSO determine whether the recorded non-existing inventories were already consumed/issued/distributed to the end-user or lost by an accountable officer. In case of loss, recognize receivable and demand payment from the accountable officer who should have custody of these inventories; 

b. Municipal Accountant make necessary adjustments to derecognize non-existing inventories without prejudice to other necessary actions in case of loss; and 

c. Inventory Committee conduct physical count of inventories semestrally, prepare RPCI, reconcile the results with the records of the Municipal Accountant, and review and analyze the cause of discrepancy for timely recognition of expenses and necessary adjustment by the Municipal Accountant, if warranted.

In addition, we also recommended that the: 

d. Municipal Accountant adopt the perpetual inventory method, maintain SLCs for each inventory type, and record expenses based on SSMI provided by MGSO; and 

e. General Services Officer maintain complete stock cards for all type of inventories to properly account the receipt and issuance of inventories, provide SSMI (at least weekly) to the Accounting Office for recording purposes, and conduct regular reconciliations with accounting records to resolve discrepancies and make necessary adjustments, if warranted.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant analyze the transactions, trace back all the intra-agency transactions and the corresponding entries made to those accounts, and eventually prepare the necessary adjustments to reconcile the intra-agency receivable and payable account balances.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant make necessary restatements of all material prior period errors identified and adjusted during the year and include the required disclosure in the Notes to FS in accordance with IPSAS 3.

We reiterated our previous recommendation that the Municipal Accountant closely coordinate with the Municipal Budget Officer in the preparation of the SCBAA and prepare the SCBAA and make necessary disclosures in accordance with the requirements of IPSAS 24.

a. Mandatory periods for some procurement activities are still not thoroughly adhered to.
We reiterated that the BAC strictly adhere to the different periods provided by RA 9184 and its 2016 Revised IRR within which certain stages of the procurement process must be completed, specifically the conduct of pre-bid conference, execution of the contract, and issuance of NTP.
We also recommended that the BAC and TWG provide explanation for the non-posting of NOA and NTP in PhilGEPS and the non-submission of proof that the NOA was posted at any conspicuous place in the premises of the Procuring Entity.

b. Contract documents and other supporting documents were either incomplete, improperly executed or unsubmitted.
We recommended that the BAC, Municipal Engineer, Municipal Accountant, and other concerned officials submit the lacking documents and/or provide valid explanations for the observed deficiencies for further audit and technical evaluation, and henceforth, ensure complete and proper documentation of each procurement project.

c. Surety bonds without the required Insurance Commission (IC) certification are still being accepted as security
We reiterated our previous recommendation that the BAC not accept surety bond as a form of security/warranty under Sections 27.2(c), 39.2(c), 62.2.3.3(c) and Annex E(4.2) of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184 unless it is supported by the required certification from the IC.

d. BAC used inappropriate/inapplicable Philippine Bidding Documents (PBD)
We recommended that the BAC justify the use of PBD for Goods for procurement involving infrastructure projects, and henceforth, meticulously prepare bid documents to ensure their applicability with the procurement at hand.
We also recommended that the BAC utilize the PBD Builder to minimize errors in the preparation of bidding documents.

e. Laxity in the preliminary evaluation of bids and post-qualification made by the BAC and its Technical Working Group (TWG)
We recommended that the BAC provide a valid written justification on why the deficiencies in the submitted eligibility and technical documents of the winning bidders were not discovered and the contract was still awarded to them. Henceforth, carefully examine the documents submitted by the bidder with the Lowest Calculated Bid during post-qualification in compliance with Section 34.3 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184.

f. There is no approved CSHP at the time of signing the contract or even prior to commencing work on several projects.
We recommended that the BAC submit an explanation on noted deficiencies and for allowing contractors to proceed with project implementation despite the absence of the approved CSHP, which should have formed part of the contract.

g. Suspension and resume orders were incomplete and not supported with complete documentation
We recommended the Municipal Engineer to provide necessary supporting documents and justification to establish the validity of the issuance of the suspension and resume orders in question; and henceforth, carefully evaluate the validity and existence of the cause and reason for any work suspension and diligently monitor the same for purposes of issuance of the corresponding resume order.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: 

a. Municipal Mayor, through the Human Resource Management Office, include in the contracts of JO/COS personnel whether they were allowed to go on official travel and the allowable amount of traveling expenses that they may claim, if authorized, which should be limited to the ceiling granted under EO 77 or any other existing travel law at the time of payment of the claim; 

b. Municipal Mayor create written internal guidelines setting forth the conditions and other controls over the travels of Municipal officials and employees, including JO/COS, with due consideration of the requirements of EO 77 and COA Resolution No. 2021-004; and 

c. Municipal Accountant ensure that, in addition to the documentary requirements under COA Circular No. 2012-001, claims for travel of JO/COS personnel are supported by: an approved Travel Request/Travel Order that clearly and specifically states the purpose of the travel and likewise contains the corresponding entitlements, duly supported by an approved itinerary of travel to provide basis for payment of traveling expenses, and a Certification from the Head of the Agency or any authorized approving official that: (i) the official missions/tasks cannot be performed by/or assigned to any other regular/permanent official and/or employee, and (ii) the tasks/activities are necessary to fulfill the obligations as contained in his/her contract of service.

We recommended that the Inventory Committee and GSO (i) exert extensive effort in accomplishing the RPCPPE and PPE-related reports to be used in application of insurance of properties; and Teams to do the tasks -selected key personnel will attend an Orientation on One-time Cleansing of PPE Account Balances on September 17, 2025 (ii) prepare the Property Inventory Form (PIF) listing all insurable assets, properties and interests of the Municipality following the guidelines and format prescribed in COA Circular No. 2018-002 dated May 31, 2018 and submit the same to GSIS, copy furnished the Audit Team, on or before April 30 of each year as basis for the assessment of general insurance coverage.

We reiterated our recommendation that the: 

a. Municipal Mayor evaluate and consider the establishment of RC for GFPS and ensure that the created RC will be able to monitor and properly account for GAD funds, as well as facilitate the generation of reliable reports pertaining to GAD-related expenses and other GAD-related financial transactions as prescribed in Item 4.1.C.8 and Item 7.0 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC Nos. 2013-01 and 2016-01 respectively; and 

b. GFPS: 

i. Complete and submit approved GAD Agenda as well as continuously update the GAD database to include complete and reliable data requirements pursuant to Annex B of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 as basis for the preparation of the annual GPB; 

ii. Inform the DILG-Provincial Office if there are any changes made to the endorsed GPB in compliance with the provisions under JMC No. 2013-01, as amended, and properly reflect the same in the GAD AR; 

iii. Submit GAD GPB and GAD AR within the prescribed period, or five working days upon receipt of GPB endorsement and five working days from the end of January of the preceding year, respectively; and 

iv. Include specific references, such as check or disbursement voucher number and other particulars, in the list of expenditures supporting the GAD AR and ensure the completeness and correctness thereof.

To facilitate the monitoring and audit of GAD AR related expenses, encode/record in the logbook of all vouchers in a monthly basis.

We reiterated our previous recommendations that the: 

a. Municipal Accountant (i) analyze the Due to BIR account and identify the causes and details of unsettled balances, (ii) if found to be pertaining to unremitted taxes, cause the immediate remittance of the same; however, any penalties and surcharges should be personally borne by persons held responsible for the late remittance; (iii) if found to be due to accounting errors, make necessary adjustments duly supported with relevant documents establishing the error; and (iv) ensure that remittances tally with the taxes withheld for the period and that all taxes withheld during the month be remitted on time; and 

b. Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer ensure that proper control and segregation of duties are in place in the recording, preparation of schedules, filing, and payment of taxes due to the BIR, observe the proper and timely remittance of taxes withheld and strictly comply with BIR RR No. 2-98 dated April 17, 1998, as amended by RR Nos. 14-2002 and 10-2008 dated September 9, 2002 and July 8, 2008, respectively.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant analyze the balances of the Due to GSIS, Due to Pag-IBIG, and Due to Philhealth accounts and reconcile their records with the concerned agencies to facilitate the remittance of the balances or adjustments to their records, as warranted.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant (a) make necessary adjustment to reflect the unutilized balance of BGCM amounting to ₱1,032,374.97 at year-end, (b) restate accordingly the CY 2020 Statement of Financial Performance and Statement of Financial Position, and (c) facilitate the preparation of year-end trial balance and financial statements prescribed in the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I for the BGCM-SAGF for CY 2020.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our previous recommendation that the Municipal
Accountant conduct detailed review and checking of bank records/reports and
request the necessary documents to support the recording of the reconciling
items, if any, and exert effort to substantiate the identified book reconciling
items and prepare the necessary adjustments immediately.

b. Non-restoration of stale and cancelled checks in CIB accounts – ₱1,186,827.37
Per Section 59, Volume I of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, checks may
be cancelled when they become spoiled or stale. A check is stale if it has been
outstanding for over six months from date of issue. Also, the same section provides
the following guidelines on the reporting of spoiled or stale checks:
A spoiled or stale check shall be marked cancelled on its
face and reported as follows:
1. For spoiled checks which are immediately cancelled
and for which the Report of Checks Issued (RCI) has
not yet been prepared, the cancelled check shall be
attached to the RCI and reported chronologically with
the other checks issued and the word “Cancelled” shall
be indicated on the report.
2. For stale checks which have been unclaimed and thus,
the original DV and supporting documents are still with
the Treasurer, the cancelled check shall be presented in
the RCI after the last check issued for the period
indicated in the report. The original DV and supporting
documents shall be returned to the Accountant who
shall prepare a JEV to record the transaction as
Accounts Payable.
3. For checks which became spoiled or stale in the hands
of the payee and which require replacement, a new
check may be issued upon submission of the spoiled or
stale check to the Treasurer. A certified copy of the DV
shall be requested from the Auditor for presentation to
the Administrator/Local Chief Executive who shall
countersign the check. The cancelled check shall be
reported and attached to the RCI prepared at the
period of cancellation. The replacement check shall
also be reported chronologically in the RCI.
Despite previous audit recommendations, it was noted that, based on the
schedule submitted by the Accounting Office, there were still 102 checks totaling
₱1,182,827.37 issued from 2015 to 2021 that were already stale but still reported as
part of the outstanding checks (with discrepancy of ₱70,000.00).
Year No. of Checks Total Amount
General Fund
2015 3 ₱ 4,550.00
2016 6 28,530.51

2019 12 93,188.11
2020 16 95,753.17

PhilHealth Capitation Fund
2020 1 46,990.18
Total 102 ₱ 1,182,827.37
Of these stale checks noted, it was observed that seventy-six (76) checks
totaling ₱771,052.26 remained outstanding and unadjusted in the accounting records
for more than two (2) years as of December 31, 2021.
Section 97 of Presidential Decree No. 1445 also states that a check which
remains outstanding after two (2) years from date of its issue shall not be paid by the
Treasurer of the Philippines or by any duly authorized depository. The issuing agency
shall take up in its books of accounts the amount of this check as surplus adjustment
of the fund against which it was previously charged. Upon presentation of a stale
warrant or check, the issuing agency shall cancel it and issue to the payee a substitute
warrant or check in lieu thereof.
Failure to restore the amount of foregoing stale checks in the books renders
the CIB account understated by ₱1,182,827.37 likewise the Accounts Payable and the
Government Equity by ₱411,775.11 and ₱771,052.26, respectively.
In addition, the list of outstanding checks includes Check No. 714525 dated
August 10, 2021 amounting to ₱4,000.00 which turned out to be a cancelled check
per submitted Report of Checks Issued. Consequently, the same understates the CIB
account and the corresponding expense account by ₱4,000.00.
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant
review the long outstanding/stale checks and require the Municipal Treasurer to
cancel any unclaimed stale checks and furnish the Accountant with the original
DVs and its supporting documents for the preparation of the JEV following the
procedures prescribed under the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs to ensure
recording of the cancellation.
For those stale checks that remain outstanding for more than two years,
we recommended the Municipal Accountant to take up in its books of accounts
the amounts of these checks as surplus adjustment of the fund against which
they were previously charged in accordance with Section 97 of PD 1445.
We also recommended the Municipal Accountant to revert the recorded
cancelled check, investigate any discrepancies in the reconciling items and draw
the necessary adjustment.

c. Unreleased checks (excluding stale) as at year-end not reverted in the CIB
account – ₱82,619.11
The Municipal Accountant still failed to reverse the amount of unreleased
checks at year-end amounting to ₱82,619.11. According to the accounting personnel,
they were not furnished with the list of unclaimed checks at year-end resulting in the
non-reversal of the account. The list of unclaimed checks, as reported by the
Municipal Treasurer upon the auditor’s request, consists of fourteen (14) checks that
were issued from October 11 to December 29, 2021.
As mentioned in the previous audit observation, generally, the preparation of
financial statements is on accrual basis except for transactions otherwise accounted
for as required by law. (Philippine Application Guidance 1, International Public
Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS) 1)
IPSAS 1, Presentation of Financial Statements defines the accrual basis as a
basis of accounting under which transactions and other events are recognized when
occurred. This means that the transactions and events are recorded in the accounting
records and recognized in the financial statements of the periods to which they relate.
Accordingly, one of the financial statement assertions of Management is that
transactions were recorded within the correct reporting period which is referred to as
Cut-off assertion. This means that effects of transactions, other events, and conditions
are faithfully represented as of the date of the financial statements.
However, under the accrual basis, all unreleased checks at the end of the year
shall be reverted to the cash accounts. This is in line with the tenet that no payment is
made until the check is received by the payee; hence the amount should still form part
of the cash in bank while the liability should still be recognized. As such, A JEV shall
be prepared to recognize the restoration of the unreleased checks to cash equivalent
and the recognition of the appropriate liability/payable account.
Hence, the Cash-in-Bank and the corresponding liability accounts were
understated by ₱82,619.11.
We also reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Treasurer prepare
the list of unclaimed checks at the end of the year and submit the same to the Municipal
Accountant for preparation of JEV to restore the amount of unreleased checks to
Cash-in-Bank, recognize the appropriate liability account every end of year, and reverse
the same in the ensuing year.

d. Insufficiently supported credit adjustments to Cash-Local Treasury
As observed in prior year, there was an unreconciled difference amounting to
₱179,499.95 between the balances of accounting ledger for Cash in Local Treasury
account and treasury cashbook. This difference decreased to ₱7,349.64 as of
December 31, 2021 as shown below:

Verification, however, noted that, except for JEV No. 100-2020-06-16, the
other adjustments totaling ₱97,859.72 were not sufficiently supported with
documents that would substantiate the derecognition of recorded cash on hand in the
books. The subject JEVs were only supported by a schedule showing the difference
between what has been and what should have been recorded. Evidence such as copies
of previous JEVs, official receipts, deposit slips and other necessary documents to
prove the corrected amount, were not provided, thereby casting doubt on the validity
of the adjustments made.
It should be noted that one of the assertions of the Management in the
financial statements is the existence of the account balances reported. Thus, any
balance of ‘Cash-Local Treasury’ in previous financial statements was considered
existing on hand of the Municipal Treasurer and/or other accountable officers. As
such, any credits/decrease on such balance should have been sufficiently and validly
supported with documents especially with regard to cash which is considered a high-
risk asset because of its susceptibility to theft and misappropriation.
We recommended the Municipal Accountant to submit previously drawn
JEVs, official receipts, deposit slips, or other necessary documents that would
sufficiently and validly substantiate the corrected amount of “Cash Local
Treasury” to support the credit adjustments on the said account.

e. Unidentified discrepancies between the Accounting records and Treasurer’s
Cashbook

of ₱7,349.64 between the Treasurer’s Cashbook and the Accounting records (books
of accounts), as earlier presented, is yet to be traced and analyzed by both offices.
In addition, although the Municipality was able to reconcile accounting
records and bank balances, it was noted that there was a discrepancy of ₱506,967.94

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendations that the:

Municipal Mayor create and establish an Inventory and Appraisal Committee, headed by the GSO with representatives from the Accounting, Engineering and Assessor’s Office, which will prepare a plan for the conduct of a complete physical inventory including the time frame to complete inventory count and the preparation of the report, taking into consideration the present condition and location of the properties. Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment (ARE) must be made available for fast tracing of the properties and should be updated to establish proper accountability;

OIC-GSO obtain from the Accounting Office the listing of properties per category of PPE as reference in the inventory taking;

Municipal Accountant and OIC-GSO coordinate and prepare supporting schedules to facilitate the proper reconciliation of the property accounts and establishment of actual existence, ownership and condition of the properties; and

Municipal Accountant maintain property ledger cards that will provide a complete breakdown of the amounts reflected in the financial statements, effect proper adjustments in the books for items that necessitate corrections as identified in the verification and reconciliation process, create separate subsidiary ledgers for the unreconciled account balances, and observe proper disclosure of the same in the Notes to Financial Statements for fair presentation.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare and maintain the
RPI for all Public Infrastructure Assets and trace back records of previously
completed infrastructure projects to facilitate recognition of the identified
infrastructure assets to their respective PPE accounts.

We reiterated our recommendations that the:

Municipal Mayor establish an Inventory Committee as part of the Municipality’s Local Roads Asset Management System, members of which includes, but are not limited to:

The GSO for property custodianship and reporting of the Local Road Network System;
The Municipal Engineer for technical assistance in the cost valuation and segregation of the Road Network and its components; and
Municipal Accountant for proper accounting of the Local Roads in line with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015.

Municipal Engineer prepare a schedule of the Local Roads indicating therein the cost of each Road Network Component such as road lot, road pavement, drainage and slope protection structures, and other miscellaneous structures to be submitted to the GSO and Accounting Office. Moreover, subsequent road projects of the Municipality should clearly indicate the separate cost of each road component for easy reference. The Engineering Office is also vested to determine the life of the Local Roads taking into consideration the design, type of users, and volume of users of the roads. observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Mayor and MDRRMC strictly comply with the provisions of Section 22 (c) of RA 7160 and Quisumbing vs. Garcia on the utilization of lump sum appropriations by: [a] submitting the LDRRMFIP to the SB and requesting them to issue a resolution ratifying the expenditures amounting to ₱224,480.00; and [b] ensure that prior authorization from the SB is secured for all the subsequent contracts under a lump sum appropriation, otherwise, the transactions will be deemed illegal pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-003.
We recommended and the Management agreed that the MDRRMC evaluate the PPAs under the unexpended GF-LDRRMF capital outlay and TF-DRRM, and reprogram the fund as deemed necessary to improve disaster resiliency of the Municipality, and include the same in the LDRRMFIP to establish its propriety in accordance with COA Circular No. 2012-002. Moreover, the MDRRMC, in the conduct of planning, [a] properly classify the PPAs according to its nature of disbursement; [b] provide specific, measurable, achievable, realistic, and time-bound performance indicators to facilitate proper assessment of the DRRM capacity of the Municipality; and [c] provide specific time-frame for the implementation of PPAs to facilitate strategic execution and monitoring of disaster-responsive measures programmed under the LDRRMFIP.
We recommended that the MDRRMO maintain an organized inventory of DRRM equipment, supplies and materials for proper safekeeping and to effectively monitor functionality and availability of the same and to provide a valid distribution list to account the exact amount of the pre-positioned goods and MDRRMC Resolution/s declaring necessity of the release of the stock piled goods, otherwise, the distribution would be deemed irregular under COA Circular No. 2012-003.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by Management.
observations.reason: No justificatio n nor validated distribution list was provided by the MDRRMO for the distribution of relief goods amounting ₱99,480.00.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the: Municipal Mayor create and establish an Inventory and Appraisal Committee, headed by the GSO with representatives from the Accounting, Engineering and Assessor’s Office, which will prepare a plan for the conduct of a complete physical inventory including the time frame to complete inventory count and the preparation of the report, taking into consideration the present condition and location of the properties. Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment (ARE) must be made available for fast tracing of the properties and should be updated to establish proper accountability; OIC-GSO obtain from the Accounting Office the listing of properties per category of PPE as reference in the inventory taking; and thereafter, Municipal Accountant and OIC-GSO coordinate and prepare supporting schedules to facilitate the proper reconciliation of the property accounts and establishment of actual existence, ownership and condition of the properties; Municipal Accountant prepare and maintain the RPI for all Public Infrastructure Assets and trace back records of the completed Infrastructure Assets starting from the current year backwards. Additionally, prepare separate subsidiary ledgers for the determined cost of the Road Network Components, and thereafter provide for the depreciation of each Road Component based from the records to be prepared by the Engineering Office to establish proper valuation of the Road Network.
